mappings.rationaleAI: The An Lushan Rebellion of 755, its devastation of Tang central authority, displacement of millions, acceleration of provincial military autonomy, and reshaping of frontier defence policy correspond directly to A.1.a on the late T'ang rebellion and its effects on provincial separatism and attempts to restore central authority.

Li Bai's Daoist lyrics and Du Fu's poet-historian documentation of suffering during the An Lushan Rebellion represent the pinnacle of T'ang cultural life, corresponding to A.1.b on growing cultural influences, developments in music and visual arts, and the literary dimensions of A.1.a's political upheaval.

Genghis Khan's unification of Mongol peoples, innovative military organisation, and conquest of the Chin empire (1211-34) correspond directly to B.1.a on Genghis Khan's conquest of the Chin and the establishment of Mongol rule over China.

Marco Polo's extended residence at Kublai Khan's court and his detailed account of Asian commerce, geography, and Mongol governance correspond to B.2.b on commercial and cultural contacts between Yuan China and the West, and B.1.b on the Mongol capital at Ta-tu and expansion of trade.

Written by Luo Guanzhong in the 14th century during the Yuan-Ming transition, this foundational classical novel blending historical record with Confucian moral themes corresponds to B.2.a on developments in the arts under Mongol rule and C.1.c on literature and cultural life in the Ming period.

Zheng He's seven maritime expeditions (1405-1433) projecting Ming imperial power to Southeast Asia, India, and East Africa, and their abrupt termination due to eunuch-Confucian bureaucrat disputes, correspond directly to C.1.b on developments in Ming foreign relations and economic policy.

mappings.episodeTitle: Matteo Ricci and the Ming Dynasty
mappings.rationaleAI: Ricci's introduction of European cartography, astronomy, and mathematics to Ming officials through intellectual exchange corresponds to C.1.c on Ming philosophy and scholarship, C.2.a.ii on early contacts with the West, and C.2.b.iii on introduction of Western sciences.

The Wall's major reconstruction with brick and stone under the Ming Dynasty specifically against Mongol incursions corresponds to C.1.a on Ming political and military structure and C.1.b on foreign relations and defence policy.

Joseph Needham's investigation of Chinese innovations including gunpowder, printing, and the compass — achievements of the Sung through Ch'ing periods — corresponds to A.3 on Sung invention of printing and manufacturing advances, and C.2.b.iii on the comparative scientific trajectory between China and the West.

Chinese imperial eunuchs wielded significant political power in Ming court governance; the eunuch-bureaucrat rivalry shaped both internal administration (C.1.a.ii on government innovations) and foreign policy, including the termination of the Ming Voyages (C.1.b).

Tea's Chinese origins and the explosive growth of Sino-British trade that ultimately drove the opium trade correspond to C.2.b.i on expansion of crafts, industries, commerce, and finance under the mid-Ch'ing, and C.2.a.ii on contacts with the West.
